author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

title: Homily XVI

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This Homily was delivered on the occasion of the Prefect entering the Church, for the purpose of pacifying the minds of the people, in consequence of a rumour of an intended sack having been announced to him, when all were meditating flight.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It treats also on the subject of avoiding oaths, and on the words of the Apostle, “Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I commend the Prefect’s consideration, that seeing the city agitated, and every one purposing a flight, he hath come here and afforded you consolation, and hath led you to entertain favourable hopes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But for you I blushed, and was ashamed, that after these long and frequent discourses ye should have needed consolation from without.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I longed that the earth would open and swallow me up, when I heard him discoursing with you, alternately administering comfort, or blaming such ill-timed and senseless cowardice.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it was not becoming, that you should be instructed by him; but you ought yourselves to be teachers to all the unbelievers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Paul did not permit even going to law before the unbelievers; but thou, after so much admonition of our Fathers, hast needed teachers from without; and certain vagabonds and miscreants have again unsettled this great city, and set it upon flight.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: With what eyes shall we hereafter look upon the unbelievers, we who were so timid and cowardly?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: With what tongue shall we speak to them, and persuade them to exercise courage as to approaching evils, when we became through this alarm more timid than any hare?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “But what could we do,” says some one, “we are but men!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This is indeed the very reason why we ought not to be terrified, because we are men, and not brutes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For these are scared by all manner of sounds and noises; because they have not reasoning power, which is adequate to dispel fear.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But thou who hast been honoured with the gift of speech and reason, how is it that thou sinkest to their ignoble condition?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hath some one entered the city, and announced the march of soldiers against it?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Be not terrified, but leaving him, bend the knee: call upon thy Lord: groan bitterly, and He will keep off the dreaded event.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hadst heard indeed a false report of the march, and wert in danger of being severed from the present life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But that blessed Job, when the messengers came one after another, and he had heard them announcing their dreadful news, and adding thereto the insupportable destruction of his children, neither cried nor groaned, but turned to prayer, and gave thanks to the Lord.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Him do thou too imitate; and when any comer announces that soldiers have encircled the city, and are about to plunder its wealth, flee to thy Lord and say, “The Lord gave, the Lord hath taken away; as it seemeth good to the Lord, so is it done.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Blessed be the name of the Lord for ever.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The experience of the actual events did not terrify him; yet the mere report frightens thee.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And how are we to be accounted of, who when we are commanded boldly to encounter death itself, are thus affrighted by a false rumour!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The man who is bewildered constructs fear which is unreal; and trouble which is not visible; but he who abides in a settled and tranquil condition of soul, breaks in pieces even that which is real.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou not pilots; when the sea is raging, and the clouds are rushing together, and the thunders are bursting forth, and all on board are in confusion, they seat themselves at the helm without tumult or disturbance; giving earnest heed to their own art, and considering how they may ward off the effects of the approaching storm.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Be these thy example; and laying hold of the sacred anchor, the hope that is in God, remain unshaken and immoveable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, which built his house upon the sand; and the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell, and great was the fall of it.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou that it is the character of folly to fall down headlong, and to be overthrown?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or rather, we were not only reduced to the condition of that foolish man, but our fall was still more wretched.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the house of that man fell down after the rivers and rains had descended, and the winds had beaten upon it; but we, when there were no winds striking, nor floods invading, nor blasts assaulting, before the experience of disaster, were overturned by a mere rumour, and dropped at once all the philosophy we were meditating.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What think ye are now my thoughts?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How should I conceal,—yea, bury myself?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How must I blush with shame?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If I had not been forcibly urged by our Fathers, I would not have arisen, I would not have spoken, whilst my mind was darkened with sadness because of your pusillanimity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But neither now have I been able to recover myself; since anger and sorrow have laid such seige to my soul.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For who would not feel provoked and indignant, that after so much teaching ye should need the instructions of Gentiles, that ye might be comforted and persuaded to bear in a manly way the present alarm.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Pray ye therefore that free utterance may be given us in opening our mouth; and that we may be able to shake off this sadness, and to hold up again a little; for indeed this shame on account of your pusillanimity hath greatly depressed our spirits.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Lately, I addressed to your Charity many things concerning the snares lying on all sides of us; and concerning fear and sadness, sorrow and pleasure; and also concerning the sickle that flieth down upon the houses of swearers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Now, out of all these many matters, I would have you especially to remember what I said respecting the “winged sickle,” and its settling in the swearer’s house; and pulling down the stones and the wood, and consuming the whole mass.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And withal, take heed to this; that it is the extreme of folly to swear by taking the Gospels, and to turn the very Law which forbids swearing into an oath; and that it is better to suffer loss of property than to impose an oath on our neighbours; since this is a great honour to be done to God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when thou sayest to God, “For thy sake I have not put such a one, who hath robbed and injured me, on his oath,” God will pay thee back a great recompense on account of this honour, both here and hereafter.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Say these things to others, and observe them also yourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I know that in this place we become more reverent, and lay aside every evil habit.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what is to be aimed at is, not that we be lovers of wisdom here only, but that when we depart, we may take this reverence out with us, where we especially need it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For those who carry water do not merely have their vessels full when near the fountain, and empty them when they reach home, but there they put them away with especial caution, that they may not be overturned, and their labours rendered useless.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us all imitate these persons; and when we come home, let us strictly retain what has been spoken; since if ye here have gotten full, but return home empty, having the vessels of your understandings destitute of what ye have heard, there will be no advantage from your replenishment here.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Shew me not the wrestler in the place of exercise, but of actual contest; and religion not at the season of hearing, but at the season of practice.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou applaudest what is said now.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When thou art required to swear, then remember all these things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If ye quickly accomplish this law, we will advance our teaching to other and greater things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Lo! this is the second year that I am discoursing to your Charity; and I have not yet been able to explain a hundred lines of the Scriptures.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the reason is, that ye need to learn of us what ye might reduce to practice at home, and of yourselves; and thus the greater part of our exhortation is consumed on ethical discourse.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But this ought not to have been so; the regulation of manners you ought to have learnt at home, and of yourselves; but the sense of the Scriptures, and the speculations upon them, you might commit to us.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If, however, it were necessary that you should hear such things of us, there was no need of more than one day: for what there is to be said is of no diversified or difficult character, or such as requires any elaboration.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when God declares His sentence, subtle arguments are unseasonable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: God hath said, “Thou shalt not swear.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do not then demand of me the reasons of this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is a royal law.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He who established it, knows the reason of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If it had not been profitable, He would not have forbidden it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Kings bring in laws, and not all perchance profitable; for they are men, and cannot be competent to discover what is useful, like God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless, we obey them.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whether we marry, or make wills, or are about to purchase servants, or houses, or fields, or to do any other act, we do these things not according to our own mind, but according to the laws which they ordain; and we are not entirely at liberty to dispose of the things which concern ourselves according to our own minds; but in many cases we are subject to their will; and should we do any thing that is contrary to their judgment, it becomes invalid and useless.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So then tell me, are we to pay so much respect to the laws of men, and trample under foot the law of God?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What defence, or what pardon can such conduct be worthy of?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He hath said, “Thou shalt not swear.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In order that thou mayest do and speak all things with safety, do not in practice lay down a law contrary to His.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But enough of these matters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us now proceed to lay before you one sentence of those which have been read to-day, and thus end this discourse.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ,” saith he, “and Timothy the brother.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Great is the designation of Paul: no title of principality and power, but he speaks of bonds and chains!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Truly great indeed!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Although many other things made him illustrious; his being caught up into the third heaven, his being transported to Paradise, his hearing unutterable words; yet he sets down none of these, but mentions the chain instead of all, for this made him more conspicuous and illustrious than these.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Because the one were the free gifts of the Lord’s lovingkindness; and the other the marks of the constancy and patience of the servant.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But it is customary with those who love, to glory more in the things which they suffer for those who are beloved, than in the benefits they receive from them.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: A king is not so proud of his diadem, as Paul gloried in his chains.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And very justly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For a diadem affords but an ornament to the crowned head; but the chain is a much greater ornament as well as a security.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The kingly crown often betrays the head it encircles, and allures innumerable traitors, and invites them to the lust of empire.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And in battles this ornament is so dangerous, that it must be hidden and laid aside.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hence kings in battle, change the outward dress, and so mingle in the crowd of combatants; so much betrayal does there result from the crown; but the chain will bring nothing of this kind upon those who have it, but altogether the contrary; since if there be a war, and an engagement with demons, and the hostile powers; the man who is thus encompassed, by holding forth his chain, repels their assaults.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And many of the secular magistrates not only bear the name of office while they are in authority, but when they have given up their authority.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Such a one is called an ex-consul, such a one an ex-prætor.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But he, instead of all such titles, says, “Paul the prisoner.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And very rightly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For those magisterial offices are no complete evidences of virtue in respect to the soul; for they are to be purchased by money, and obtained by the solicitations of friends; but this distinction that is obtained by bonds is a proof of the soul’s love of wisdom, and the strongest sign of a longing for Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the former are soon gone, but this distinction has none to succeed to it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Behold at least from that time to the present day how long a time has passed, and yet the name of this Prisoner has become increasingly illustrious.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As to all the consuls, whoever they were, of former times, they are passed into silence; and not even their names are known to the generality of mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the name of this Prisoner, the blessed Paul, is still great here, great in the land of the barbarians, great also among the Scythians and Indians; and were you to go even to the very bounds of the habitable world, you would hear of this appellation, and whithersoever any one could come, he would perceive that the name of Paul was borne in the mouths of all men.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what marvel is it, if it be so by land and sea, when even in the heavens the name of Paul is great; with angels and archangels and the powers above, and with the King of these, even God!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “But what were the chains,” says some one, “that brought glory to him who was thus fettered?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Were they not formed of iron?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Of iron, indeed, they were formed; but they contained the grace of the Spirit, abundantly flourishing in them; since he wore them for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Oh, wonder! the servants were bound, the Master was crucified, and yet the preaching of the Gospel every day increases!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And through the means by which it was supposed that it would be extinguished, by these very means it was kindled; and the Cross and bonds, which were thought to be an abomination, these are now become the symbols of salvation; and that iron was to us more precious than all gold, not by its intrinsic nature, but for this cause and ground!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But here I see an enquiry arising out of this point; and if you give me your attention, I will both state the question exactly, and will add the solution.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then is the subject of enquiry?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This same Paul once having come before Festus, whilst discoursing to him, and defending himself concerning the charges which the Jews had alleged against him, and telling how he had seen Jesus, how he had heard that blessed voice; how he had been struck with blindness and recovered sight, and had fallen down and risen up again; how he had come a captive into Damascus, bound without chains; after speaking likewise of the Law and of the Prophets, and shewing that they had foretold all these things, he captured the judge, and almost persuaded him to come over to himself.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For such are the souls of holy men: when they have fallen into dangers, they do not consider how they may be delivered from dangers, but strive every way how they may capture their persecutors.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Just so did it then happen.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He came in to defend himself, and he departed taking the judge with him!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And to this the judge bore witness, saying, “Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this ought to have happened to-day; and this Prefect, on coming among you, ought to have admired your magnanimity, your fortitude, your perfect tranquillity; and to have gone away, taking with him a lesson from your good order, admiring your assembly, praising your congress, and learning from the actual fact, how great a difference there is between Gentiles and Christians!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But as I was saying:—When Paul had caught him, and he said, “Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian,” Paul answered thus, “I would to God that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost and altogether such as I am, except these bonds.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What sayest thou, O Paul?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When thou writest to the Ephesians, thou sayest, “I therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you, that ye walk worthy of the vocation wherewith ye are called.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And when thou speakest to Timothy, “Wherein I suffer trouble as an evil-doer, even unto bonds.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, when to Philemon, thus; “Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, when debating with the Jews, thou sayest, “For the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And writing to the Philippians, thou sayest, “Many of the brethren in the Lord, waxing confident by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Every where thou bearest about the chain, everywhere thou puttest forward thy bonds, and boastest in the thing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when thou comest to the tribunal, thou betrayest thy philosophy, where it were right to have spoken the most boldly, and sayest to the judge, “I would to God that thou mightest become a Christian ‘without’ these bonds!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Yet surely if the bonds were good, and so good, that they could be the means of making others to grow bold in the cause of true religion; (for this very thing thou didst declare before, when thou saidst, “Many of the brethren, waxing confident by my bonds, did speak the word without fear”); for what reason dost thou not glory in this thing in the presence of the judge, but doest even the reverse?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Does not what I say appear a question?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The solution of it, however, I will bring forward at once.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For Paul acted thus, not from distress or fear, but from an abundance of wisdom and spiritual understanding.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And how this was, I proceed to explain.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He was addressing a Gentile, and an unbeliever, who knew nothing of our matters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hence he was unwilling to introduce him by way of disagreeable things, but as he said, “I became to them that are without law, as without law;” so he acted in the present instance.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: His meaning is, “If the Gentile hear of bonds and tribulations, he will straightway be taking flight; since he knows not the power of bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: First, let him become a believer; let him taste of the word preached, and then he will even of himself hasten towards these bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I have heard the Lord saying, “No man putteth a piece of new cloth into an old garment, for that which is put in to fill it up taketh from the garment, and the rent is made worse.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Neither do men put new wine into old wine-skins; else the wine-skins burst.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The soul of this man is an old garment: an old wine-skin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is not renewed by the faith, nor renovated by the grace of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is yet weak and earthly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It affects the things of this life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It flutters eagerly after worldly show.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It loves a glory that is present.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Should he hear at once, even from the first, that if he becomes a Christian he will become immediately a prisoner, and will be encompassed with a chain; feeling ashamed and indignant, he will recoil from the word preached.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore, saith he, “Except these bonds.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Not as deprecating the bonds themselves, God forbid!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But condescending to the other’s infirmity; for he himself loved and welcomed his bonds, even as a woman fond of ornament doth her jewels of gold.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whence is this apparent?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “I rejoice,” saith he, “in my sufferings for you, and fill up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ in my flesh.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again; “Unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ, not only to believe on Him, but to suffer for His sake.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again; “And not only so, but we also glory in tribulations.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Wherefore, if he rejoices and glories in this, and calls it a gift of grace, it is manifest that when he was addressing the judge, he spoke to him as he did, for the reason assigned.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Moreover, also in a different passage, when he happened to find a necessity for glorying, he shews the very same by saying, “Most gladly, therefore, will I glory in my infirmities……in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses, that the power of Christ may rest upon me.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again; “If I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And elsewhere, comparing himself with others, and exhibiting to us his superiority in the comparison, he thus speaks; “Are they ministers of Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: (I speak as a fool), I am more.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And wishing to shew this superiority, he did not say that he had raised the dead, nor that he had expelled demons, nor that he had cleansed lepers, nor that he had done any other thing of the sort, but that he had suffered those innumerable hardships.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hence when he said,” I am more,” he presently cites the multitude of his trials; “In stripes, above measure, in deaths oft, in prisons more frequent…..of the Jews five times received I forty stripes save one, once was I stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have been in the deep;” and all the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus Paul everywhere glories in tribulations; and prides himself upon this circumstance exceedingly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And very justly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this it is which especially shews the power of Christ, viz. that the Apostles conquered by such means; by bonds, by tribulations, by scourgings, and the worst of ills.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For these two things Christ had announced, tribulation and remission, labours and crowns, toils and rewards, things pleasant and sad.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless, to the present life he assigns the sorrowful things; but for the life to come, he has stored up those which are pleasant; at once shewing that He did not mean to deceive men, and wishing by this arrangement to diminish the burden of human woes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the imposter first holds out the things which are pleasant, and afterwards brings forward those which are disagreeable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus for example:—Kidnappers, when they intend to steal and carry off little children, do not promise them blows and stripes, or any other thing of that kind, but offer them cakes, and sweetmeats, and such like, by which the age of childhood is usually gratified; in order that, enticed by these things, they may sell their liberty, and may fall into the utmost peril.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Moreover, bird-catchers, and fishermen, thus entice the prey which they pursue, offering first their usual food, and such as is agreeable to them, and by this means concealing the snare.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So that this is especially the work of imposters, first to hold out things which are agreeable, but afterwards to introduce the things which are disagreeable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the case is altogether the reverse with those who are really careful and provident for others.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Fathers at least act quite in a contrary manner to kidnappers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When they send their children to school, they set masters over them, threaten them with stripes, and encompass them with fear on all sides.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when they have thus spent the first portion of their lives, and their habits are formed, they then put them in possession of honour, and power, and luxury, and all the wealth that is theirs.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And thus God has acted.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: After the manner of provident fathers, and not after that of kidnappers, He has first involved us in things that are grievous; handing us over to present tribulation, as it were to schoolmasters and teachers; in order that being chastened and sobered by these things, after shewing forth all patience, and learning all right discipline, we may afterwards, when formed into due habits, inherit the kingdom of heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He first prepares and fits us for the management of the wealth He is to give, and then puts us into the actual possession of riches.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if He had not acted thus, the giving of riches would have been no boon, but a punishment and a vengeance.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For even as a son that is senseless and prodigal, when he has succeeded to a paternal inheritance, is precipitated headlong by this very thing, having none of the practical wisdom requisite for the economy of wealth; but if he be intelligent, and gentle, and sober, and moderate, managing his paternal estate as is befitting, he becomes by this means more illustrious and distinguished: so must it also necessarily happen in our case.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When we have acquired spiritual understanding, when we have all attained to “perfect manhood,” and the measure of full stature;” then He puts us in possession of all that He has promised: but now as little children He chastens us, together with consolation and soothing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this is not the only advantage of receiving the tribulation beforehand, but there is also another, not less than this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the man who first of all lives luxuriously, and then has to expect punishment after his luxurious living, has not even a sense of his present luxury, merely by reason of the expectation of impending woes; but he who is first in a sorrowful state, if he is anticipating the enjoyment of good things afterwards, overlooks present difficulties, in the hope of the good things which are to come.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Not only, then, on account of our security, but also for our pleasure and consolation hath He ordained that the things which are grievous should be first; in order that being lightened with the hope of futurity, we should be rendered insensible to what is present.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this Paul would shew and make plain, when he said, “Our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He calls tribulation light, not because of the intrinsic nature of things that are grievous, but because of the expectation of good things to come.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For even as the merchant is indifferent to the labour that attends navigation, being buoyed up with the hope of a cargo; and as the boxer bravely sustains the blows on his head, looking to the crown beyond; so also indeed do we, earnestly gazing towards heaven, and the good things that are in the heavens, whatever evils come on us, sustain them all with fortitude, being nerved with the good hope of the things to come.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore let us go home, taking with us this saying; for though it be simple and short, it nevertheless contains much of the doctrine of spiritual wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He who is in a state of grief and tribulation, hath a sufficient consolation; he who lives in luxury and abundance, hath that which may greatly sober him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when as thou sittest at the table thou art reminded of this saying, thou wilt speedily shrink from drunkenness and gluttony; learning through this sentence, how needful it is for us to be striving; and thou wilt say with thyself, “Paul lived in bonds and in dungeons, but I in drunkenness and at a luxurious table!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What pardon then shall I obtain?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This also is a fit saying for women; since those who are fond of ornament, and expensive dresses, and bind themselves about with gold on every side, when they remember this chain, will hate, I feel assured, and abominate that adorning of themselves; and will hasten to such bonds as these.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For those ornaments have often been the cause of manifold evils, and introduced a thousand quarrels into a family, and have bred envy, and jealousy, and hatred.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But these loosed the sins of the wide world, affrighted demons, and drove away the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: With these, while tarrying in prison, he persuaded the jailor; with these he attracted Agrippa himself; with these he procured many disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore he said, “Wherein I suffer trouble as an evil-doer unto bonds, but the word of God is not bound.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For just as it is not possible to bind a sunbeam, or to shut it up within the house, so neither the preaching of the word; and what was much more, the teacher was bound, and yet the word flew abroad; he inhabited the prison, and yet his doctrine rapidly winged its way every where throughout the world!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Knowing these things then, let us not be depressed, when adverse affairs meet us, but then let us be more strong, then more powerful; “for tribulation worketh patience.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not grieve for the calamities which befall us, but let us in all things give thanks unto God!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We have completed the second week of the fast, but this we should not consider; for going through the fast does not consist in merely going through the time, but in going through it with amendment of manners.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us consider this; whether we have become more diligent; whether we have corrected any of our defects; whether we have washed away our sins?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is common for every one to ask in Lent, how many weeks each has fasted; and some may be heard saying that they have fasted two, others three, and others that they have fasted the whole of the weeks.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what advantage is it, if we have gone through the fast devoid of good works?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If another says, “I have fasted the whole of Lent,” do thou say, “I had an enemy, but I was reconciled; I had a custom of evil-speaking, but I put a stop to it; I had a custom of swearing, but I have broken through this evil practice.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is of no advantage to merchants, to have gone over a great extent of ocean, but to have sailed with a freight and much merchandise.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The fast will profit us nothing, if we pass through it as a mere matter of course, without any result.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If we practise a mere abstinence from meats, when the forty days are past, the fast is over too.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if we abstain from sins, this still remains, even when the fast has gone by, and will be from this time a continual advantage to us; and will here render us no small recompense, before we attain unto the kingdom of heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For as he who is living in iniquity, even before hell, hath punishment, being stung by his conscience; so the man who is rich in good works, even before the kingdom, will have the benefit of exceeding joy, in that he is nourished with blessed hopes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore Christ says, “I will see you again, and your heart shall rejoice, and your joy no man taketh from you.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: A brief saying, but one that hath in it much consolation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then is this, “your joy no man taketh?” if thou hast money, many are able to take away the joy that comes of thy wealth; as, for instance, a thief, by digging through the wall; a servant by carrying off what was entrusted to him; an emperor by confiscation; and the envious man by contumely.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Should you possess power, there are many who are able to deprive you of the joy of it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when the conditions of office are at an end, the conditions of pleasure will also be ended.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And in the exercise of office itself too, there are many accidents occurring, which by bringing difficulty and care, strike at the root of thy satisfaction.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If thou hast bodily strength, the assaults of disease put a stop to joy from that source.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If thou hast beauty and bloom, the approach of old age withers it, and takes away that joy.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or if thou enjoyest a sumptuous table, when evening comes on the joy of the banquet is at an end; for every thing belonging to this life is liable to damage, and is unable to afford us a lasting pleasure; but piety and the virtue of the soul is altogether the reverse of this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If thou hast done an alms, no one is able to take away this good work.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Though an army, or kings, or myriads of calumniators and conspirators, were to beset thee on all sides, they could not take away the possession, once deposited in heaven; but the joy thereof continually abideth; for it is said, “He hath dispersed, he hath given to the poor, his righteousness endureth for ever.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And very justly; for in the storehouses of heaven it is laid up, where no thief breaks in, nor robber seizes, nor moth devours.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If thou pourest out continued and fervent prayers, no man will be able to spoil thee of the fruit of them; for this fruit too is rooted in the heavens; it is out of the way of all injury, and remains beyond mortal reach.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If when evil-treated thou has done a kind action; if thou hast borne with patience to hear thyself evil spoken of; if thou hast returned blessings for reproaches; these are good works that abide continually, and the joy of them no man taketh away; but as often as thou rememberest these, thou art glad and rejoicest, and reapest large fruits of pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So also, indeed, if we succeed in avoiding oaths; and persuade our tongue to abstain from this pernicious practice, the good work will be finished in a short time, but the delight arising from it will be continuous and unfailing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And now, it is time that you should be teachers and guides of others; that friends should undertake to instruct and lead on their neighbours; servants their fellow-servants; and youths those of their own age.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What if any one had promised thee a single piece of gold for every man who was reformed, wouldest thou not then have used every exertion, and been all day long sitting by them, persuading and exhorting.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Yet now God promises thee not one piece of gold, nor ten, or twenty, or a hundred, or a thousand; no, nor the whole earth, for thy labours, but He gives thee that which is greater than all the world, the kingdom of heaven; and not only this, but also another thing besides it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what kind of thing is that?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “He who taketh forth the precious from the vile,” saith He, “shall be as my mouth.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What can be equal to this in point of honour or security?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What kind of excuse or pardon can be left to those, who after so great a promise neglect their neighbour’s safety?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Now if you see a blind man falling into a pit, you stretch forth a hand, and think it a disgraceful thing to overlook one who is about to perish?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But daily beholding all thy brethren precipitated into the wicked custom of oaths, dost thou not dare even to utter a word?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast spoken once, perhaps, and he hath not heard.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Speak therefore twice, and thrice, and as often as it may be, till thou hast persuaded him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Every day God is addressing us, and we do not hear; and yet He does not leave off speaking.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do thou, therefore, imitate this tender care towards thy neighbour.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason it is that we are placed with one another; that we inhabit cities, and that we meet together in churches, in order that we may bear one another’s burdens, that we may correct one another’s sins.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And in the same manner as persons inhabiting the same shop, carry on a separate traffic, yet put all afterwards into the common fund, so also let us act.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whatever advantages each man is able to confer upon his neighbour, let him not grudge, nor shrink from doing it, but let there be some such kind of spiritual commerce, and reciprocity; in order that having deposited every thing in the common store, and obtained great riches, and procured a large treasure, we may be all together partakers of the kingdom of heaven; through the grace and loving-kindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, by Whom and with Whom, to the Father, with the Holy Ghost, be glory, both now and ever, and world without end.
